coredata.dc:title: A novel biomass carbon dots ratiometric fluorescence probe coupled with molecularly imprinted polymer for selective detection of aflatoxin B1
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Jiangbin
Yang, Wei
Liu, Yuanfa
coredata.dc:description: The accumulation of Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) in food and crops can pose significant hazards to human health. Consequently, there has been extensive attention paid to the development of a rapid and reliable method for detecting AFB1 in food. In this context, a ratiometric fluorescence probe (DE-MIPs) was prepared using dual-emission fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) coupled with the molecular imprinting technique for visual detection of AFB1. The dual-emission CDs consisted of red fluorescence biomass CDs (R-CDs) wrapped by molecularly imprinted cavities (R-CDs@MIPs), which served as a response signal, and pure blue emission biomass CDs (B-CDs) as a reference signal. Due to the specific quenching effect of AFB1 on red fluorescence while leaving blue fluorescence unaffected, there was a gradual decrease in the fluorescence intensity ratio (I670/I437) and a distinct shift in fluorescence color from red to blue as the concentration of AFB1 increased. The ratiometric probe demonstrated excellent linearity in detecting AFB1 within the concentration range of 0.1–200 ng/mL, with a detection limit (LOD) of 0.05 ng/mL. Furthermore, the practicality of DE-MIPs was validated by successfully detecting AFB1 in vegetable oil, with desirable recoveries from 89.0% to 107.4%. Furthermore, the integration of a smartphone as colorimetric reader enabled rapid AFB1 detection by capturing changes in fluorescence color. Overall, the development of this ratiometric probe offers a novel perspective for the facile and efficient identification of AFB1 in complex food samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass carbon dots
Aflatoxin B1
Ratiometric fluorescence probe
Molecular imprinting polymers
Visual detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization and potential sensing mechanism of the DE-MIPs probe
Selectivity and anti-interference analysis of DE-MIPs sensor
Application of DE-MIPs sensor in vegetable oil

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the structural framework of dummy template imprinting technique and the preparation of R-CDs@MIP and DE-MIPs. (a) The molecular structure of AFB1 and the specific locations of DMC fragment; (b) The mechanism of molecular imprinting and recognition of the AFB1 by the dummy fragment; (c) The synthetic procedure and (d) mechanism of R-CDs@MIPs; (e) The preparation of DE-MIPs fluorescence sensing system and corresponding detection application.

Characterization of DE-MIPs. (a) Fluorescence spectra of B-CDs, R-CDs, and R-CDs@MIPs; (b) Fluorescence spectra of DE-MIPs before and after adding AFB1; (c) TEM and HRTEM image of R-CDs; (d) TEM image of R-CDs@MIPs; (e) SEM image of R-CDs; (f) SEM image of R-CDs@MIPs; (g) FT-IR spectra of R-CDs, R-CDs@MIPs, and R-CDs@NIPs; (h) UV–Vis absorption spectra of R-CDs, R-CDs@MIPs, R-CDs@NIPs, and DMC; (i) XRD pattern of the R-CDs and R-CDs@MIPs.

Sensing mechanism research and schematic diagram. (a) Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs and R-CDs@MIPs with or without the addition of AFB1 and DMC, respectively; (b) Fluorescence lifetime spectra of R-CDs before and after adding AFB1; (c) Schematic diagram of fluorescence quenching mechanism and corresponding system energy level.

Conditional optimization results. (a) Fluorescence intensity ratio (I670/I437) of the R-CDs@MIPs probe and B-CDs under different ratio (3:1, 2:1, 3:2, 1:1, 1:2, and 1:4) and different AFB1 concentrations (0, 50, 100, 150, and 200 ng/mL); (b) The corresponding fluorescent images; (c) the effects of pH and (d) reaction time on the performance of DE-MIPs probe.

Sensitivity analysis of the R-CDs@MIPs and DE-MIPs probe. (a) Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs@MIPs probe adding various concentrations of AFB1 (0–200 ng/mL), the inset: the corresponding fluorescence images at different concentrations of AFB1 (λex = 365 nm); (b) Linear relationship between the logarithm of AFB1 concentration and fluorescence intensity; (c) Fluorescence spectra of the DE-MIPs probe adding various concentrations of AFB1 (0–200 ng/mL), the inset: the corresponding fluorescence images at different concentrations of AFB1 (λex = 365 nm); (d) Linear relationship between the logarithm of AFB1 concentration and I670/I437.
Schematic diagram illustrating the visual detection procedure for AFB1 integrated with a smartphone-assisted readout platform.
The determination of AFB1 spiked in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A novel biomass carbon dots ratiometric fluorescence probe coupled with molecularly imprinted polymer for selective detection of aflatoxin B1

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Future Food (Bai Ma) Research Institute, 111 Baima Road, Nanjing, 211200, Jiangsu, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The accumulation of Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) in food and crops can pose significant hazards to human health. Consequently, there has been extensive attention paid to the development of a rapid and reliable method for detecting AFB1 in food. In this context, a ratiometric fluorescence probe (DE-MIPs) was prepared using dual-emission fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) coupled with the molecular imprinting technique for visual detection of AFB1. The dual-emission CDs consisted of red fluorescence biomass CDs (R-CDs) wrapped by molecularly imprinted cavities (R-CDs@MIPs), which served as a response signal, and pure blue emission biomass CDs (B-CDs) as a reference signal. Due to the specific quenching effect of AFB1 on red fluorescence while leaving blue fluorescence unaffected, there was a gradual decrease in the fluorescence intensity ratio (I670/I437) and a distinct shift in fluorescence color from red to blue as the concentration of AFB1 increased. The ratiometric probe demonstrated excellent linearity in detecting AFB1 within the concentration range of 0.1–200 ng/mL, with a detection limit (LOD) of 0.05 ng/mL. Furthermore, the practicality of DE-MIPs was validated by successfully detecting AFB1 in vegetable oil, with desirable recoveries from 89.0% to 107.4%. Furthermore, the integration of a smartphone as colorimetric reader enabled rapid AFB1 detection by capturing changes in fluorescence color. Overall, the development of this ratiometric probe offers a novel perspective for the facile and efficient identification of AFB1 in complex food samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), a highly toxic and carcinogenic secondary metabolite, is synthesized by Aspergillus flavus and Aspergillus parasiticus fungi (Huang et al., 2022). It is a matter of significant public and governmental concern due to its exceptional stability in agricultural crops and processed food (Xue et al., 2019). The consumption of food contaminated with AFB1 by humans can result in the bioaccumulation of toxins within the body, which in turn impairs the synthesis of DNA and RNA and subsequently disrupting cellular division (Zhou et al., 2019). The disruption may result in both immediate and long-term toxic effects, contributing to the development of birth defects (Lu et al., 2023). The accumulation of AFB1 in liver cells is particularly pronounced, thereby augmenting the susceptibility to liver cancer (Wang et al., 2020). In order to ensure food safety, various countries have established maximum permissible levels for AFB1 in vegetable oils. The European Commission currently sets the maximum allowable level of AFB1 in edible oils at 2 μg/kg, while China has specifically set limits for peanut and maize oils at 20 μg/kg (Yu et al., 2019). To date, a number of methods have been developed for the detection of AFB1, including liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) (Meng et al., 2019) enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (Lu et al., 2024), surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) (Wang et al., 2018), electrochemical sensing techniques (Li et al., 2024), colorimetry and fluorometric method (Kong et al., 2024). These detection methods possess high sensitivity and specificity in detecting AFB1. Among them, fluorescence detection techniques have gained increasing attention in the fields of analytical chemistry due to their inherent advantages, including ease of operation, rapid response, visualization capabilities, and excellent sensitivity (Xu et al., 2023). Various types of fluorescent markers and nanoparticles, including metal–organic frameworks (MOFs), quantum dots (QDs), and metal nanoclusters, have been employed for the detection of AFB1 using fluorescence signal mechanisms based on turn-on or turn-off principles. For example, the aptamer-conjugated CdTe/ZnS were used as fluorescence indicators for detection of AFB1 using the fluorescent technique (Chi & Liu, 2023). Hong et al. synthesized an automated fluorescent immunosensor for sensitive detection of aflatoxin B1 based on copper-based metal–organic framework (Cu-MOF) (Hong et al., 2024). However, single-signal emitting probes are not only susceptible to external factors but also have limited visual identification capabilities (Liu et al., 2019). Ratiometric fluorescence technology, which depends on the ratio of multiple emission intensities, has been extensively employed in the construction of fluorescent sensors. In contrast to a single fluorescence, it possesses the ability to mitigate external interference through self-calibration, thereby enhancing detection sensitivity (Liu, Lin, et al., 2024). Moreover, it has the capacity to generate a wide range of colors with varying degrees of intensity, thereby enhancing visual perception (Zhu et al., 2023). The variation in color can be employed to quantify the concentration of a target substance using portable devices such as smartphones. This is achieved by utilizing the signal strength ratio of the three primary colors, such as R/G (Sun et al., 2022).

Carbon dots (CDs), zero-dimensional fluorescent nanomaterials, have been employed in a multitude of applications, including biochemical sensing and medical imaging (Liu, Lin, et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2024). Their distinctive features, such as tunable fluorescence, exceptional stability, facile surface functionalization, and efficient analyte capture capabilities (Ezati et al., 2023; Li et al., 2021), have contributed to their widespread utilization in fluorescence detecting filed (Ji et al., 2023). Recently, the potential of utilizing various waste biomass substances as cost-effective carbon sources for synthesizing CDs has been acknowledged. During the carbonization process, these organic materials can generate CDs that are enriched with oxygen and nitrogen functional groups on their surface (Zhang et al., 2018). However, there is no existing literature on using smartphone supported ratiometric biomass CDs-based fluorescence probes for the visual monitoring of AFB1 levels. Furthermore, the assessment of fluorescence probe performance should strive for exceptional specificity towards the target compound (Wang et al., 2022). The molecular imprinting technology (MIT) employs the capacity of template molecules to retain their functional group and shape as a memory, enabling the identification of a target (Liu, Lin, et al., 2024). Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have been employed in a multitude of complex matrices for the specific recognition of target compounds due to their possession of distinct recognition sites (Cheubong et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2024). In MIPs-fluorescence sensors, the detection of the target analyte involves the interaction between fluorescent nanoprobe and analyte within imprinted cavities, resulting in a modification of fluorescence emission intensity (Li et al., 2023). Compared to conventional fluorescence probes, the combination of MIPs and sensors could provide a discerning recognition component, facilitating the identification of targets in the complex food matrices (Kumar et al., 2018). Consequently, a ratiometric fluorescence probe was successfully constructed by integrating biomass CDs and MIPs for the purpose of detecting AFB1 in real samples.

Herein, we have developed a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probe (DE-MIPs) for AFB1 detection by integrating a molecular imprinting strategy with biomass CDs. The red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs) were coated with a sol-gel derived molecularly imprinted layer to form R-CDs@MIPs, which exhibited remarkable selectivity towards AFB1. The R-CDs@MIPs were subsequently mixed with blue-emission CDs (B-CDs) at an appropriate ratio to fabricate the DE-MIPs probe. Upon the inclusion of AFB1, the emission of R-CDs@MIPs decreased as a response signal due to photo-induced electronic transfer (PET), whereas the B-CDs remained unchanged as an interior reference. As a result, there was a reduction in the fluorescence intensity ratio (I670/I437). It is noteworthy that the alteration of I670/I437 was also manifested by a fluorescence color transition from red to purple to blue when exposed to a 365 nm ultraviolet (UV) lamp, enabling visual identification of AFB1 with the naked eye. Additionally, when integrated with a smartphone-based color recognition application, real-time capture and analysis of fluorescence information facilitated on-site quantification of AFB1. Therefore, the sensing platform developed in this research not only represents a novel approach for detecting AFB1 but also establishes a credible and convenient monitoring platform in the field of food safety.

Acetone, ethanol, 5,7-dimethoxycoumarin (DMC), methacrylic acid (MAA), ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EDMA) and 2,2-azobisisobutyonnitrile (AIBN) were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Petroleum ether, ethyl acetate, acetic acid and PBS buffer solution (pH = 7) were obtained from Titan Technology Co., (Shanghai, China). Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), aflatoxin B2 (AFB2), aflatoxin G1 (AFG1), aflatoxin G2 (AFG2), ochratoxin A (OTA) and zearalenone (ZEN) were obtained from Macklin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The maize oil and peanut oil were bought from a local market. All the reagents employed in this study were of analytical quality and were used in their original state.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a JEM-ARM200F field-emission electron microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained using Quanta 250 FEG microscope (FEI, USA). A D8 ADVANCE (XRD, Bruker, Hamburg, Germany) was used to conduct the X-ray diffractometer. A Vertex 70 spectrometer (Bruker, Karlsruhe, Germany) was employed for scanning Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR). The UV-1750 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan) was employed to carry out the UV–visible absorption spectra analysis. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using the Thermo Escalab 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Massachusetts, USA). The fluorescence spectra were gathered using a HITACHI F-7000 spectrophotometer (HITACHI, Tokyo, Japan). The analysis of fluorescence lifetime was performed using QuantaMaster 40 Fluorescence Lifetime Spectrometers (PTI, Arizona, USA).

The fresh hibiscus leaves were dried at 60 °C for 5 h in a vacuum oven, and then smashed into powder using a high-speed pulverizer. The resulting hibiscus leaf powder (1.5 g) was placed in a centrifuge tube containing 30 mL of acetone solution and subjected to treatment for 30 min. Next, the solution was placed in Teflon-lined autoclave and solvothermal treated at 120 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature (25 ± 2 °C), a crude solution was obtained using 0.22 μm filtration membrane. The obtained crude solution was finally purified through column chromatography with the mixture of ethyl acetate and petroleum ether. The collected green solution was distillated to remove the eluent, the obtained CDs powder was dissolved in ethanol reserved as stock solution.

The walnut shells were dried at 60 °C for 5 h in a vacuum oven, followed by pulverization using a high-speed pulverizer to obtain powdered walnut shells. Subsequently, 1.5 g of the resulting powder was uniformly dispersed in a Teflon -lined stainless steel reactor containing 30 mL of DMF, which was maintained at 200 °C for 8 h. The resulting solution was filtered through 0.22 μm membranes and subsequently underwent dialysis for 48 h (3500 Da). Finally, brown CDs were obtained by freeze-drying the dialysate.

The R-CDs@MIPs were synthesized using a sol-gel technique similar to the reported process with minor modifications. In a 30 mL acetone solution, a mixture containing 2 mL of R-CDs stack solution, 0.6 mmol of template, and 0.9 of mmol MAA was prepared and stirred at room temperature for 120 min. Next, 1.2 mmol of EDMA was introduced and fully mixed for 15 min. After achieving uniform dispersion, 10 mg AIBN was added in the reaction system, and stirred for 24 h at 60 °C in the dark. The MIPs-coated R-CDs were then centrifuged and washed with a mixture of ethanol/acetic acid (9:1, v/v) to eliminate DMC. The R-CDs@MIPs were flashed with distill water and dried at 45 °C in vacuum oven for further experiment. The non-imprinted material (R-CDs@NIPs) was synthesized using the same procedure but without adding template molecular.

The dual-emission sensing system was obtained by thoroughly integrating 350 μL of R-CDs@MIPs solution (2 mg/mL) with 350 μL of B-CDs solution (0.5 mg/mL) within a test tube. The DE-MIPs ratiometric fluorescence probe was then prepared by diluting the mixture to a final volume of 2 mL with phosphate buffer saline (PBS) pH 7. For AFB1 detection, the sensor was combined with 100 μL of the AFB1 standard solution at different concentrations, and incubated at room temperature for 17 min. The fluorescence spectra of the mixed solutions were recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer with excitation at 375 nm. The quantitative analysis of AFB1 content was performed by calculating the fluorescence intensity ratio (I670/I437) between R-CDs@MIPs (I670) and B-CDs (I437). The selectivity and anti-inference of the DE-MIPs was evaluated by introducing compounds with similar structural characteristics and potentially interfering ions.

The impact of various concentrations of AFB1 on the sensing system was evaluated using a smartphone application for quantitative analysis based on changes in fluorescence color. Specially, the synthesized ratio fluorescence probe was combined with 100 μL of the AFB1 standard solution at different concentrations and incubated at room temperature for 17 min. A series of fluorescence images were captured using the smartphone (iQOO 11) while exposed to 365 nm UV radiation and subsequently converted into their respective RGB values. The B/R ratio obtained from the blue and red channels was used as an indicator in the quantification of AFB1. Then, the linear relation between the R/B ratio and the concentration of AFB1 was established. The images were obtained in darkness to avoid any potential disruption from external light sources.

The applicability of this ratiometric fluorescence probe in the analysis of agricultural food samples was assessed using peanut oil and corn oil. The oil samples obtained from a local market were subsequently spiked with AFB1 at three concentrations of 0, 1, 5, and 20 ng/mL to perform the recovery test. Next, the edible oil (0.5 mL) was mixed with methanol-deionized water solution (2.5 mL, 7:3 v/v) and subjected to ultrasound treatment (Donghe Ultrasonic Technology Co., LTD, Ningbo, China) for 30 min. After centrifugation at 10,875 g for 10 min, the obtained supernatant was filtered using 0.22 μm membrane. The extraction solutions were analyzed following the procedure described in Section 2.3. The samples containing spikes were ultimately determined using the DE-MIPs probe and the smartphone-assisted sensing platform.

The DE-MIPs probe for AFB1 detection was prepared by integrating the fluorescence recognition signal generated through molecular imprinting with a reference signal. Initially, the biomass carbon dots of red fluorescence with emission wavelength at 670 nm and blue fluorescence at 437 nm were synthesized respectively (Figs. S1 and 2a). Then, the R-CDs@MIPs probe was synthesized by incorporating R-CDs into a molecularly imprinted layer using the sol-gel technique. As depicted in Fig. 1 a, DMC exhibits a structural similarity to AFB1 with comparable molecular shape and functional group arrangements. The formation of strong hydrogen bonds was facilitated by the interaction between carbonyl functional group of DMC and the carboxyl functional group of monomers. Furthermore, the non-toxic nature and lower cost of DMC compared to AFB1 make it a favorable alternative. Therefore, the application of DMC as a suitable dummy fragment for AFB1 is more advantageous in terms of cost and security measures. Similar to antibodies that recognizing specific epitopes on antigens, the resulting MIPs could exhibit selectivity towards DMC-like components present in the structure of AFB1. The procedure of coating R-CDs with a thin layer of MIPs was depicted in Fig. 1c, and the intricate mechanism was exhibited in Fig. 1d. In the initial stage of polymerization, a preliminary bond was established between MAA and DMC. The MAA was then accumulated on the surface of the R-CDs particles owing to the hydrogen bond between the amino group and the carboxyl group. When EDMA and AIBN were introduced, a network-like gel state polymer incorporating DMC was generated and grew on the R-CDs particle under suitable conditions. The R-CDs@MIPs were successfully prepared when the eluent disrupted the hydrogen bond between the DMC and the monomer within the polymer layer, resulting in template release and formation of MIP cavities. These cavities readily reattached DMC fragment in the AFB1 molecular.

As depicted in Fig. 2 a, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs@MIPs exhibited a noticeable reduction in comparison to that of R-CDs, primarily attributed to the development of an MIPs and the creation of recognition cavities (Dai et al., 2023). The significant wavelength gap (approximately 230 nm) between red and blue would facilitate the visual detection of AFB1 through color changes. The general principle for monitoring the AFB1 through the ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform was depicted in Fig. 2b. The red emission of R-CDs@MIPs exhibited a significant decrease upon the addition of AFB1 to the sensing system, while the blue fluorescence of B-CDs remained unaltered, as expected. In the presence of AFB1, a hydrogen bond was established between the carboxyl groups within the MIP cavities and the oxygen atoms of DMC fragment. This interaction facilitated electron and energy transfer, leading to a change in the fluorescence of R-CDs@MIPs. Consequently, the quantity of the AFB1 can be determined through the change of fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs@MIPs. The phenomenon suggested the R-CDs@MIPs could serve as a response signal in ratiometric fluorescence probe, and the B-CDs was regarded as reference signal. Meanwhile, AFB1 induced an obvious color change in the DE-MIPs fluorescence sensor from red to bluish violet (the inset of Fig. 2b), enabling visual monitoring of AFB1. Compared to traditional fluorescence sensors that emit a single wavelength, the ratiometric fluorescence probe possessed enhanced precision, stability, and responsiveness.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and potential sensing mechanism of the DE-MIPs probe
ce:section.ce:para: The size and morphology of biomass CDs were examined through High resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) and SEM. The synthesized R-CDs exhibited uniformly dispersed spherical particles, as depicted in Fig. 2c and S2a, with an average diameter of 2.57 nm. The lattice spacing of R-CDs was observed to be 0.21 nm under HRTEM, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphene (Yuan et al., 2016). The TEM image of B-CDs also displayed uniform dispersion with an average particle diameter of 3.02 nm, while the lattice spacing remained indistinct (Figs. S2b and c). After coating R-CDs with MIPs layer, a significant increase in the nanoparticle size was observed compared to bare R-CDs (Fig. 2d), indicating complete encapsulation of R-CDs by the MIPs layer. The morphology of the R-CDs@NIPs exhibited similarities to that of R-CDs@MIPs, with no discernible distinctions observed (Fig. S2d). The SEM images of the R-CDs and R-CDs@MIPs samples can be observed in Fig. 2e and f, respectively. The morphology of the imprinting layer was characterized by a rough surface, while the R-CDs displayed a comparatively smooth appearance. The above results indicate that the molecular imprinting layer has been effectively incorporated. The composition and surface functional groups of the R-CDs, R-CDs@MIP, and R-CDs@NIPs samples were determined through FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2g). The characteristic peaks observed in the R-CDs were found at 3437 cm−1 and 3360 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H, respectively (Jia et al., 2019). Moreover, the presence of the C–H stretching band at 2996 and 2917 cm−1, as well as a CO stretching at 1668 cm−1, were also observed (Yang et al., 2021). The prominent peaks in the spectra indicated a high abundance of amino and carboxyl groups on the R-CDs, which facilitates strong hydrogen bond interactions with hydroxyl (–OH) groups of functional monomers (Lv & Gao, 2020). Additionally, the XPS scanning method was employed to analyze the elemental composition and chemical state of R-CDs on the surface. As shown in Fig. S3a, the full spectrum analysis revealed that the presence of C, N, and O elements at 284.8, 399.13, and 532.25 eV, respectively. Further examination of the high-resolution spectra was conducted, and the C1s spectrum was deconvoluted into three peaks at 288.37, 285.34, and 284.45 eV (Fig. S3b), which were attributed to CO, C–O, and C–C/CC, respectively. The O1s spectrum in Fig. S3c displayed distinct peaks at 533.21 eV for O–CO and 531.7 eV for C–O (Xu et al., 2022). The N1s spectrum (Fig. 3 d) exhibited two characteristic peaks at 401.39 and 399.27 eV, which correspond to the pyrrolic N and –NH group, respectively (Lu et al., 2022). On the other hand, the R-CDs@MIPs demonstrated a variety of new absorption peaks that were associated with the imprinted polymer. The absorption peaks at 1221 cm−1 and 1145 cm−1 were attributed to the vibration of the ester group and epoxy C–O–C ring, confirming the existence of EGDMA (Qin et al., 2023; Wen et al., 2023). In addition, the intensity of the C–N absorption peak at 1035 cm−1 and the absorption band around 3400 cm−1 in R-CDs@MIPs was decreased compared with R-CDs, which can be ascribed to the encapsulation of functional groups of R-CDs by the imprinting layer. Importantly, the multitude of functional groups on R-CDs can also establish interactions with the functional monomer through noncovalent forces, including van der Waals interactions, electrostatic attractions, and hydrogen bonding, thereby facilitating the formation of a strong interface between R-CDs and the polymer (Ling et al., 2022). The FT-IR spectrum of R-CDs@NIP was nearly identical to that of R-CDs@MIP, which suggested their similar chemical structures. The successful coating of imprinted materials was further confirmed by the contrasting UV–Vis spectra observed between R-CDs and R-CDs@MIPs. As shown in Fig. 2h, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of R-CDs exhibited two characteristic peaks at 417 and 660 nm, signifying the presence of chlorophyll (Tang et al., 2023). The absorbance intensity of the two peaks in R-CDs@MIPs and R-CDs@NIPs was reduced compared to unmodified R-CDs nanoparticles, owing to the phenomenon of light diffusion on the polymer layer coating the R-CDs (Deng et al., 2012). The R-CDs@MIPs showed a minor absorption band at 325 nm, which could be attributed to the residue of DMC. The crystal structures of R-CDs and R-CDs@MIPs were investigated using X-ray diffraction (XRD). The spectra of both materials exhibited broad diffraction peaks around 2θ = 21.3°, as depicted in Fig. 2i, without any impurity peaks. This peak is associated with the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, suggesting that both the R-CDs and their polymer preserve the condition of amorphous carbon (Bian et al., 2018).

The possible fluorescence quenching mechanism of R-CDs@MIPs was investigated through the structure characterization and spectral response of the material involved. The sensing system was initially assembled using R-CDs without a polymer coating, as shown in Fig. 3a, but it showed no response after the introduction of AFB1 and DMC. In contrast, the fluorescence of R-CDs@MIPs was suppressed by AFB1 and DMC, suggesting that the polymer coating on R-CDs may potentially play a mediating role in triggering the sensing process. Based on previous findings, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of AFB1 exhibited no overlap with the fluorescence spectrum of the R-CDs@MIPs (Fig. S4), excluding the possibility of internal filtration effect (IFE) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Tang et al., 2022). Additionally, the introduction of AFB1 solution led to a remarkable decrease in the fluorescence lifetime (τ value) of R-CDs@MIPs (Fig. 3b). The average τ values of R-CDs@MIPs were 12.85 ns before adding AFB1 and 11.38 ns after. These findings suggested the fluorescence quenching caused by AFB1 could be ascribed to photo-induced electronic transfer (PET) (Zhang et al., 2022). During the process of identification, the template molecule can induce a change in the average fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs@MIPs composites through hydrogen bonding interactions with the functional monomer MAA (-NH2). This interaction has the potential to bring the AFB1 into close proximity with the R-CDs, resulting to fluorescence quenching. In process of quenching, the excited state of the fluorescent substance undergoes collisions with a quencher molecule, leading to the dissipation of excitation energy and subsequent return to its initial ground state. The schematic representation depicting the sensing mechanism for AFB1 by as-synthesized R-CDs@MIPs was illustrated in Fig. 3c. Upon binding of the sensing material to the AFB1, electron transfer occurs from the donor to the acceptor. As a result, there is a reduction in the excited-state energy level of the donor, leading to a concomitant decrease in fluorescence emission.

The optimization of detection conditions was conducted to ensure the optimal operational performance of the sensing system, including synthesis conditions, pH, reaction time and the ratio of R-CDs@MIPs and B-CDs. The effectiveness and specificity of final MIPs are greatly influenced by the proportions of the template molecule, functional monomer, and cross-linker. The MIPs were prepared with varying molar proportions, and the corresponding results can be found in Table S1. The evaluation of specific recognition capability was conducted by fitting Stem-Volmer equation.
F 0
F
=
K
S V
C + 1
Where F
0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of the template, respectively; C is the target concentration; and K
SV is the quenching constant for the quencher. The ratio of K
SV, MIPs to K
SV, NIPs is defined as the imprinting factor (IF). The results indicated that the fluorescent probe based on the molar proportion of 2:3:4 exhibited the optimal recognition property. Meanwhile, the pivotal determinant for visual detection employing a ratiometric fluorescence sensor lies in the alteration of dual-emission response. The fluorescence intensity ratio (I670/I437) and fluorescent color were investigated before and after adding AFB1 to determine the optimal proportion of R-CDs@MIPs probe to B-CDs in the ratiometric sensor. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a and b, the I670/I437 ratio displayed a decline with decreasing R-CDs proportion in the absence of AFB1 supplementation, accompanied by a color transition from pink to light purple in the ratiometric probe. After the introduction of AFB1, a clear decreasing trend in the I670/I437 ratio was observed with increasing concentrations of AFB1. This phenomenon can be attributed to the gradual reduction in fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs@MIPs probe, while maintaining relatively stable fluorescence intensity of B-CDs. The fluorescence color of ratiometric sensor is primarily determined by the characteristics of fluorescent chromophores (He et al., 2018). The fluorescence color of the different proportions R-CDs@MIPs/B-CDs was investigated adding different concentrations of AFB1. When the ratio of R-CDs@MIPs probe to B-CDs was adjusted to 1:1, discernible variations in fluorescence colors were visually observed at different concentrations of AFB1 by direct naked eye. Therefore, subsequent experiments were conducted using a volume ratio of 1:1 between the R-CDs-MIPs probe and B-CDs. To achieve the best experimental outcomes, we also optimized the pH level and reaction duration. Notably, as depicted in Fig. 4c and d, the optimum pH was determined to be 7 while the ideal reaction time was identified as 17 min.

The sensitivity of the DE-MIPs probe was evaluated to assess its analytical capabilities and compared it with that of the R-CDs@MIPs single wavelength sensor. As depicted in Fig. 5 a, the gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity at 670 nm was observed as the concentration of AFB1 increased, accompanied by a quenching of monochromatic red fluorescence (Insert image of Fig. 5a). The fluorescence intensity at 670 nm and the logarithm of AFB1 concentration displayed an excellent linear relationship, which fit well into the equation: I670 = −271.51 ln CAFB1+690.61 (R2 = 0.9867) with a concentration range spanning from 0.1 to 200 ng/mL (Fig. 5b). The limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 0.08 ng/mL, employing the formula: 3δ/K (where δ represents the standard deviation of the blank and K denotes the slope of calibration curve). For the ratiometric probe, there was a gradual decline in fluorescence intensity at 670 nm and minimal change at 437 nm with increasing AFB1 concentration (Fig. 5c). The I670/I437 decreased linearly with the logarithm of AFB1 concentration, and this relationship can be expressed as: I670/I437 = −0.5246 ln CAFB1 + 1.7131 (R2 = 0.9948), within the range of 0.1–200 ng/mL (Fig. 5d). The LOD determined for DE-MIPs was merely 0.05 ng/mL, indicating the potential of the developed fluorescence sensor as a reliable approach for accurately measuring AFB1 at trace levels. According to the data presented in Table S2, the analysis performance of the DE-MIPs sensor prepared herein exhibited similarities or even surpass those of previously reported probes. Furthermore, the naked eye can easily distinguish the fluorescence color change with increasing AFB1 concentration. As depicted in the inserted image of Fig. 5c, the fluorescence color of the DE-MIPs probe under 365 nm UV illumination exhibited a gradual shift from red to purple and finally to blue as the concentration of AFB1 increased. Compared to the R-CDs@MIPs probe, the ratiometric sensing system demonstrated enhanced sensitivity with a lower detection limit and an easily observable alteration in fluorescence color. The obtained results facilitate a rapid and visually semi-quantitative determination of AFB1 using the naked eye, relying on the discernible variation in fluorescence color.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference analysis of DE-MIPs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the selectivity of the DE-MIPs sensor in detecting AFB1, we examined similar compounds such as AFB2, AFG1, AFG2, OTA and ZEN under identical experimental conditions. As depicted in Fig. S5a, the fluorescence intensity ratio of DE-MIPs exhibited a significant decrease after the adding of AFB1, while the ratiometric probe remained nearly unchanged in the presence of ZEN and OTA. Additionally, AFB2, AFG1 and AFG2 exhibited a slight quenching effect, potentially attributed to their structural similarity with AFB1 (Fig. S6). In comparison, the I670/I437 ratio of R-CDs@NIPs solution remained unchanged when adding AFB1 and other analogues. According to above results, the selectivity in the recognition process of AFB1 may arise from the synergistic involvement of functional groups and spatial conformation. The outstanding anti-interference capability of DE-MIPs is crucial for their successful application in practical scenarios, especially when analyzing food samples with intricate compositions. The introduction of common interference saline ions (Fe3+, Al3+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, K+, Na+, Ag+) did not result in any alterations in the I670/I437 ratio (Fig. S5b). In addition, the stability of the probe was also confirmed by evaluating its detection performance daily for a week. As shown in Fig. S5c, the I670/I437 ratio of ratiometric probe remained unchanged. These compelling results demonstrated that the DE-MIPs possessed remarkable selectivity and resistance to interference.

When exposed to a 365 nm UV lamp, the probe exhibited noticeable variations in fluorescence color upon addition of AFB1 at various concentrations. However, accurately determining the AFB1 content only through visual observation is impractical due to the limited ability of the naked eye to distinguish subtle variations in fluorescence color. Therefore, in response to the demand for an affordable and efficient tool in practical applications, portable devices capable of accurately performing quantitative detection are of great significance. This innovative device integrated a ratiometric probe with a widely used smartphone, serving as both a reader of fluorescence color and an analyzer of target content. The following procedures were performed (Fig. 6 ): initially, the ratio fluorescence probe was placed in a centrifuge tube and subjected to different concentrations of AFB1 for 17 min. Next, the treated solution was exposed to UV lamp with 365 nm, and the resulting fluorescent color was captured using a smartphone camera. Finally, the Color Picker application effectively transformed fluorescent color into digital values within the RGB color mode for quantitative detection of AFB1. The image captured by the smartphone exhibited a noticeable change in fluorescence color as the AFB1 levels increased. Furthermore, a linear relationship was observed between the R/B ratio and AFB1 levels (R2 = 0.9739). The gradient of the linear equation describing the correlation between R/B and AFB1 closely resembles that observed in a fluorescence spectrometer. This correspondence implies that the integration of a smartphone application into an instrument-free sensing platform enables the swift and cost-effective quantitative detection of AFB1. Therefore, the implementation of on-site and outdoor assays for AFB1 using the proposed sensing system is both feasible and advantageous.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of DE-MIPs sensor in vegetable oil
ce:section.ce:para: The practicality and reliability of the DE-MIPs sensing system for quantifying actual samples were evaluated using peanut oil and corn oil as representative samples. The standard addition method was employed at four concentration levels (spiked and 20 ng/mL), and the average recovery was calculated from triplicate measurements at each concentration. The results presented in Table 1 demonstrate that both types of oil samples exhibited satisfactory recoveries within the range of 89.0%–107.4%. Meanwhile, the effectiveness of this smart device was also assessed by converting fluorescent color of treated solution into digital values. The recoveries ranged from 86.0% to 103.6%, indicating the potential use of a smartphone platform for precise determination of AFB1 in real food. The validation of the analysis results was conducted by employing the standard High Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) technique. As observed, the results obtained from the probe demonstrated a significant concurrence with the outcomes of HPLC. Overall, the proposed method demonstrated satisfactory results for detecting AFB1 in actual samples, thus confirming its practicality and reliability.

In conclusion, a highly efficient ratiometric fluorescent probe DE-MIPs was successfully prepared and verified, using R-CDs@MIPs as a response signal and B-CDs as reference signal. In the appearance of AFB1, a reduction in red fluorescence intensity was observed for R-CDs@MIPs due to PET, while the blue fluorescence emission of B-CDs remained unaltered, which significantly improved the precision of the monitoring system. Meanwhile, the corresponding fluorescence exhibited a gradual shift in color from red to blue. The ratiometric probe demonstrated exceptional selectivity and anti-interference capability attributed to the presence of imprinted layer on the surface of R-CDs@MIPs. Meanwhile, the successful integration of the system with a mobile application for color identification has demonstrated its effectiveness in accurately measuring imperceptible color variations. This finding highlights the promising potential of this system for real-time visual quantification in field detection. The study developed a novel and valuable tool for detecting AFB1 in food analysis.
Jiangbin Xu: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Wei Yang: Validation. Yuanfa Liu: Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Key R&D Program of China (2021YFD2100300) and Project of Jiangsu Provincial Center of Technology Innovation for Future Food (BM2020023) as well as Jiangsu Specially-Appointed Professor program.
